title: Australian F2: Grunt Triumphs Through Chaos to Launch 2026 Campaign
meta: Published: 8 March 2026 | By Samira Al-Rashid, Emerging Talent Scout | F1CC News
caption: Jaromir Grunt celebrates an unexpected victory at Albert Park.
body:
Melbourne’s Albert Park hosted the 2026 F2 season opener and delivered exactly the kind of race the category specialises in — relentless, unpredictable, and with a result no pre‑race projection would have confidently called at its outset. Jaromir Grunt emerged victorious for AIX Racing, but the route to that result wound through a red flag, a safety car, a devastating teammate collision, a sudden puncture, and a ferocious multi‑car battle on the final lap. The Czech driver, entirely anonymous in practice, seized pole in a qualifying thriller and never let go, absorbing every shock the afternoon could throw at him. Mark my words: Jaromir Grunt will be an F1 driver. The only question is how soon.

Grunt’s story is the kind that makes this paddock hum. A boy from Brno who grew up karting on a tight budget, his father running a small garage, his mother working double shifts to fund a set of used tyres. He came through the ranks without the backing of a wealthy family or a major academy until Red Bull finally took notice late in his F3 campaign. This winter, he moved into the AIX Racing seat with little fanfare, quietly putting in the simulator hours while bigger names grabbed the headlines. Then Melbourne arrived. And the anonymous rookie from AIX Racing rewrote the script in a single weekend.

Practice: A Weekend of Mixed Form

Three free practice sessions produced three different pacesetters, hinting at the unpredictability to come. Takumi Aizawa (Campos Racing) set the opening benchmark in FP1 with a 1:22.576, narrowly edging Andrew Price (Prema) and Yann Asdelian (DAMS). Aizawa, a Red Bull junior with a reputation for raw speed, looked immediately comfortable around the Albert Park layout, his car dancing through the fast chicane. FP2 saw Colin Hongnoi (Rodin Motorsport) top the times with a 1:22.928, while Hamish Porter and Tim Mittens showed strong one‑lap speed. Hongnoi, the Belgian with Thai heritage, had flown under the radar in pre‑season testing, but his FP2 pace suggested a driver ready to convert promise into results. In FP3, Daniel Park (Trident) laid down an eye‑catching 1:18.117, with Mittens again second and Jacob Vavro third. Park’s lap was the kind that makes team principals sit up: committed, precise, and on the very edge of grip. Notably, Jaromir Grunt was nowhere near the top of any practice sheet, failing to crack the top ten in any session. His best effort — a 1:24.3 in FP2 — left him scratching his head, telling his engineer over the radio that the car felt “vague” in the rear. Few would have predicted what came next.

Tim Mittens, the Haas Academy driver from Oklahoma, was the picture of consistency. Top‑three in every session, his Hitech machinery looked planted in both high‑ and low‑speed corners. Mittens, who grew up on dirt ovals before switching to single‑seaters, has a racer’s instinct for finding grip where others cannot. His weekend would ultimately end in a podium celebration, but not before a gut‑wrenching Q2 elimination that tested his nerve.

Qualifying: Grunt Steals Pole by Six Thousandths

Qualifying unravelled across three tense phases, each one squeezing the field tighter. Q1 saw Park go fastest as a late surge from Tim Mittens and Kanata Rivington eliminated early favourite Andrew Price, who had topped FP1 but could not reproduce that speed when it mattered. Price, a Mercedes Academy driver with a glittering junior CV, sat in the cockpit for a long moment after the chequered flag, his helmet still on, the disappointment radiating from the Prema garage. Ryan Melon, Marek Dubišar, and Jacob Vavro also fell at the first hurdle, their weekends narrowing before the sun had climbed fully over the circuit.

Q2 tightened the screw further: Aizawa and Rivington traded fastest laps before Colin Hongnoi and Marcos Olmedo delivered knockout blows in the closing seconds, sending Mittens, Brock Lee, Daniel Park, and Alex Thompson out of the session. Mittens had been top‑three in every practice hour; to be eliminated here, by hundredths, was a cruel twist. He took off his gloves slowly, staring at the timing screen, a driver whose pace deserved more.

Q3 came down to the final thousandths. Korv Batt briefly held provisional pole, the Singaporean driver’s smooth style extracting every metre from the Prema chassis. Only for Jaromir Grunt — who had been nowhere all weekend — to produce a sensational three‑purple‑sector lap of 1:21.888. Kodiak Callahan (DAMS) came within 0.006 seconds of stealing it, a margin that haunted the Australian on home soil. Callahan, racing in front of a home crowd for the first time in F2, had dreamed of pole; to miss it by the width of a tyre tread was a wound that would not heal quickly. Marcos Olmedo and Colin Hongnoi filled row two, while Batt was left ruing a messy middle sector that cost him a shot at the front. It was a pole position that stunned the paddock and set the stage for a chaotic Sunday. Grunt, the boy from Brno who had scraped together karting funds one tyre at a time, was now the man to beat.

Race: Premeur’s Heavy Crash Halts the Action

The feature race was barely minutes old when the red flag flew. Corentin Premeur, running for ART Grand Prix, was involved in a heavy crash at Turn 5, his car left stricken and the barriers requiring repair. The Danish Red Bull junior, a pre‑race wildcard with a reputation for bold overtakes, was extracted from the cockpit by the medical team, shaken but unhurt. The incident removed one of the most exciting talents from contention and forced a full restart, compressing the field and resetting tyre strategies before any meaningful pit window had opened. In the paddock, Premeur’s ART mechanics began the long, quiet work of packing up a car that had raced only a handful of corners. He would be back, but Melbourne would not be his weekend.

At the restart, Grunt reasserted control, holding off Batt into Turn 1 and quickly building a 2.4‑second cushion. The Czech driver’s launch was perfect, his AIX Racing machine gripping the asphalt as if it had found a line no one else could see. Kodiak Callahan slotted into third, while Hamish Porter (Invicta Racing) and Tim Mittens (Hitech Pulse‑Eight) began to apply pressure. Mittens, still stinging from his Q2 exit, drove with controlled fury. He pulled off two back‑to‑back overtakes to climb into the top six, his Hitech looking racy on the medium compound, the car rotating beautifully through the slow‑speed corners. Behind them, the midfield was a restless swarm — drivers jostling for position, DRS trains forming and breaking, all while keeping an eye on the opening pit‑stop window that would reshape the race.

The racing was fierce but clean. Charles Mounir (MP Motorsport), the Audi junior from Morocco, made a brave move around the outside of Turn 3 to take ninth, his car control on cold tyres a small masterclass. Frederik Frid (Van Amersfoort Racing), a VCARB Academy driver with a quiet confidence, climbed from 12th to 8th in a single five‑lap spell, picking off cars with the patience of a veteran. Yann Asdelian (DAMS), the French Alpine protégé, was engaged in a tense battle with his teammate Callahan — the kind of intra‑team duel that makes engineers hold their breath and team principals reach for the radio.

Strategic Chess: Undercuts, Lead Changes, and a Puncture

The pit window fractured the order completely. Porter and Callahan were among the first to blink, triggering an undercut battle that reshaped the podium fight. Porter’s stop was swift and decisive — 2.3 seconds stationary, a near‑perfect release — vaulting him ahead of Callahan and into a net second place. The Invicta crew, working with the precision of a team that has honed its pit‑stop drills over a long winter, delivered the moment of the race for their driver. Grunt stayed out longer, stretching his lead to nearly seven seconds before pitting and emerging just ahead of Batt — who then suffered a sticky wheel gun issue that cost precious seconds and dropped him down the order. Batt’s Prema mechanics, usually so reliable, could only watch as the race slipped away.

As the field cycled through, late‑stoppers Jacob Vavro and Colin Hongnoi briefly inherited the lead, the pair gambling on track position over fresh rubber. For a moment, the timing screens showed a one‑two for AIX Racing and Rodin Motorsport, a snapshot of what‑might‑be. But the gamble unravelled. Hongnoi suffered a sudden puncture shortly after his stop, a devastating blow that destroyed his afternoon. The Belgian pulled over with a flattened rear tyre, his race ending in a cloud of smoke and frustration. Vavro’s ambitious strategy ended in retirement when he crashed out in the final third of the race — though not before setting the outright fastest lap of the race, a searing 1:19.182, a bittersweet footnote for AIX Racing. The lap, carved in clean air after his stop, was a glimpse of raw speed that will be remembered long after the DNF is forgotten.

Safety Car Carnage: Campos Teammates Collide

The race’s decisive chapter arrived with a sickening thud. Under pressure in the midfield, Campos Racing teammates Takumi Aizawa and Marcos Olmedo made contact, the two cars tangling and spearing into the barriers. The impact was heavy, carbon fibre scattering across the exit of Turn 9. Both were eliminated instantly, the collision ending a weekend that had promised so much for the Spanish team. Aizawa, fastest in FP1, had been a genuine threat — his car quick enough to fight at the front, his confidence high after a strong pre‑season. Olmedo had qualified fourth, his Ferrari Academy backing a mark of the sport’s faith in his future. Instead, the team left Melbourne with a double DNF and a paddock full of questions. The safety car was deployed, erasing Grunt’s commanding 9.5‑second lead and bunching the field for a sprint to the finish.

At the restart, the tension was palpable. Grunt led Porter, with Callahan in third — but Tim Mittens had other ideas. The American, still carrying the sting of his Q2 exit, lunged past Callahan at Turn 3 to snatch the final podium position, his Hitech darting to the inside with a move that required absolute commitment. The Haas Academy driver refused to let go, his car planted, his nerves steady. Korv Batt, charging back from his pit‑stop delay, then sliced past Callahan for fourth — a move born of desperation and a car that was finally in clean air. Yann Asdelian (DAMS) pulled off the uncomfortable move of passing his own teammate Callahan for fifth, the Frenchman’s audacity a mark of a driver who will not wait for invitations. Callahan’s race had unravelled from 0.006 seconds off pole to a lonely seventh, his home hopes dashed in a handful of corners. The Australian sat in the cockpit after the flag, staring ahead, a race that had promised so much leaving him with little but the echo of what might have been.

Final Lap Shootout: Mittens Holds Off the Pack

The final lap was a classic F2 knife‑fight. Mittens found himself under relentless pressure from Batt and Asdelian, the three cars running almost nose‑to‑tail through Albert Park’s fast sweepers. Batt looked to the inside at Turn 9, his Prema edging alongside the Hitech, but Mittens — ice‑cool under braking — placed his car perfectly, shutting the door without crossing the line. Asdelian probed around the outside at Turn 11, his DAMS flashing in the American’s mirrors, but again Mittens held firm, his exit speed out of the corner just enough to keep the Frenchman at bay. They crossed the line in a blur of colour and spray, Mittens just ahead of Batt, securing his first podium of the season. Asdelian finished a valiant fifth, his late‑race aggression a promise of more to come. Daniel Park (Trident) collected solid points in sixth, a strong start for the Mercedes Academy driver, while Frederik Frid and Ryan Melon rounded out the points, their quiet efficiency a reminder that consistency pays.

Grunt’s Masterclass: From Anonymous to Victorious

Jaromir Grunt crossed the line 8.877 seconds clear of Porter, a margin that flattered the chaos behind him. The AIX Racing driver had led every lap of the restarted race, managing the red flag, the safety car, and two restarts with a composure that belied his rookie status. It was a lights‑to‑flag victory that absorbed every shock without ever looking threatened. Combined with pole position, Grunt leaves Australia with a perfect 25‑point haul and the championship lead — a position that seemed unthinkable when practice ended on Saturday. The boy from Brno, the boy whose father once sold a family car to buy a kart engine, now sits atop the F2 standings. Mark my words: this is only the beginning.

Winners and Losers

Winners: Jaromir Grunt — from zero practice pace to pole and a dominant win. His story is the kind that fuels this paddock. Hamish Porter — a smart, composed drive and a well‑executed undercut gave Invicta Racing a strong second. The Williams Academy driver is building a reputation for racecraft. Tim Mittens — top‑three in every practice session, out in Q2, and a last‑lap podium rewarded relentless pace. Mittens, the dirt‑oval kid turned F2 podium finisher, is a name you will hear more of. Frederik Frid and Daniel Park — quiet but effective drives into the points, promising building blocks for the season.

Losers: Campos Racing — a double DNF after a teammate collision, an unmitigated disaster. The team had pace all weekend; to leave with nothing is a wound that will sting all the way to Bahrain. Kodiak Callahan — 0.006s from pole, ended seventh and was passed by his own teammate. A nightmare on home soil. Andrew Price — fastest in FP1, out in Q1. Colin Hongnoi — a puncture robbed him of a likely points finish. Corentin Premeur — the red flag was a brutal end to a weekend that never got started.

What They Said

Jaromir Grunt (1st): "I wasn’t on anyone’s radar after practice — honestly, neither was I. But the car just came alive in qualifying, and today I just had to hit my marks and not get caught up in the madness. The restarts were tense, but the team kept me calm. To win from pole in my first race of the season is a dream. My father is watching from Brno — this one is for him."

Hamish Porter (2nd): "We played the strategy perfectly. The undercut was the key — I knew if I could jump Callahan, I could control the gap. I’m a little disappointed we couldn’t challenge Jaromir for the win, but 18 points is a fantastic way to start the year."

Tim Mittens (3rd): "Consistency all weekend paid off. The last lap was insane, three‑wide into Turn 3 at one point. I just focused on hitting my apexes and not giving them a sniff. This podium tastes sweet after getting knocked out in Q2. I grew up racing on dirt — you learn to hold your line when there’s chaos around you. That helped today."

Takumi Aizawa (DNF, Campos): "A nightmare ending. We had the pace all weekend — I was quickest in practice — but one moment of misjudgment with my teammate and it’s over. I’m sorry to the team. We have to make sure this never happens again."

Kodiak Callahan (7th): "0.006 seconds from pole and then to finish seventh is a tough pill. The restart didn’t help, and I just didn’t have the pace on the mediums. We’ll bounce back in Bahrain. Racing in front of my home crowd, I wanted more — for them and for me."

Championship Outlook

Grunt leads the drivers’ standings with 25 points, followed by Porter on 18 and Mittens on 15. The field’s density at the top — Batt (12), Asdelian (10), and Park (8) are all within striking distance — suggests this lead will be fiercely contested. Invicta Racing’s Porter took a commanding second, while Van Amersfoort Racing’s Frid also scored well in eighth, giving both teams early‑season points and confidence. Campos will be desperate to reset after a scoreless and bruising opener. The paddock now packs up and heads to Bahrain, where the desert heat and a very different circuit will ask new questions. The 2026 F2 season, it is already clear, will be a brawl from start to finish. And somewhere in that brawl, a new star will emerge — perhaps the same one who just conquered Melbourne, perhaps someone we haven’t yet seen. That’s the magic of Formula 2. That’s why we watch.
